figures.caption: Fig. 5: Comparison of cache behavior for random circuits (RC) using LMDB and Redis backends. Y axis is the total number of sucircuits simulations done.
Fig. 3: Comparison of cache behavior for HEA circuits using LMDB and Redis backends. Y axis is the total number of sucircuits simulations done.
TABLE V: Storage growth for LMDB and Redis backends. Full results store complete statevectors (wire cutting), while compact storage retains only expectation values (QAOA).

Fig. 1: End-to-end workflow of the Quantum Circuit Cache. Incoming circuits are translated into ZX-calculus graphs, reduced via a deterministic rewrite strategy, deterministically serialized, and hashed using Weisfeiler–Leman refinement. Cache hits bypass execution and retrieve stored results, while cache misses trigger execution on CPU, GPU, or QPU backends and insertion into a persistent key–value store.

Fig. 6: Cumulative cache hits versus DE iteration for QAOA depths p = 2, 3, 4 under coarse, medium, and fine parameter discretization. Cache reuse increases steadily over time, with coarser and medium discretizations exhibiting higher hit rates.

Fig. 7: Best Max-Cut energy versus DE iteration for different QAOA depths and discretization resolutions. Energies converge rapidly and saturate within a narrow range due to equivalence-aware circuit evaluation.
TABLE I: Comparison of database backends used in the Quantum Circuit Cache.
TABLE IV: Final cache statistics after the full DE run (population 500, 50 iterations).
